# The Architecture 2.0 Moonshot {#sec-moonshot}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The purpose of computing is insight, not numbers."*
>
> — Richard Hamming, *Numerical Methods for Scientists and Engineers* (1962)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Richard Hamming, a pioneer in computer science, famously emphasized that computing should yield insight rather than raw data. For this chapter, the quote anchors the book's core premise. AI-assisted architecture should improve how architects formulate questions, test alternatives, explain mechanisms, and defend decisions. Candidate volume alone is not progress.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter introduces the Architecture 2.0 moonshot. It begins with a compact design request and shows how an architect can develop it into a bounded AI-assisted study with alternatives, executable artifacts, experiments, a mechanism-level explanation, and a defensible decision.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What would it take for AI to help an architect formulate, explore, implement,
evaluate, explain, and defend a system design, including the evidence needed to
judge any candidate it generates?
:::

A compact prompt is not yet an architecture study. It becomes useful when the
architect expands it into a scoped question, meaningful alternatives,
checkable artifacts, experiments, a mechanism-level explanation, and a
recommendation whose limits another architect can challenge. AI may assist
each part of that work. It may also produce plausible fragments faster than a
team can connect them to a decision supported by evidence. When the work
iterates, recording the design loop lets another architect follow the movement
among those parts. The loop supports the study; it is not the goal.

The moonshot therefore requires more than generation capacity. A system that
can propose a thousand accelerators but cannot test why they differ, explain
why candidates failed, or state what evidence would overturn the survivor has
not advanced architecture very far. It has only increased the number of
proposals that must be checked. Cheap candidate generation is useful only when
the team can also reject unsupported designs quickly. A fast analytical model might,
for example, prune any accelerator that exceeds the thermal budget before the
team spends scarce simulator or physical-design time on it.

The running prompt illustrates that pressure.

> **Lighthouse Prompt:** Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives.

The chapter returns to decompose this prompt once its requirements are
in view; for now it stands as the fixed target a study must eventually
answer.

Computer architecture builds hardware-software systems from workload intent,
technology constraints,
software assumptions, physical limits, and evidence. It has never been only about which
tools you use; it is about how well you build, holding efficiency, safety,
robustness, and reliability to evidence rather than to assertion. Architecture
2.0 names the next step in that practice. AI can participate across
formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense,
but it remains grounded in architectural representations, tools, experiments,
and human judgment [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20].

The practical reason architecture needs this practice is efficiency, but
efficiency no longer means a single
performance number. The classical quantitative tradition already treated
performance, cost, and power as coupled architectural questions
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Today, efficiency includes performance, energy,
power delivery, reliability, scalability, sustainability, cost, verification
burden, engineering effort, and time to evidence strong enough for a decision. Across that range,
the design problem couples hardware, software, tools, and deployment.
This multidimensional coupling is exactly what makes naive generation
insufficient. An AI-assisted study must state which constraints, tools,
experiments, and evidence can change its architectural claim. Recording the
loop makes that work executable rather than leaving it implicit.

For roughly fifty years, processor
generations improved through a remarkably stable loop. Device scaling delivered
faster, cheaper, lower-power transistors on a regular schedule; the
quantitative method turned design choices into measured comparisons; when
scaling slowed, microarchitecture, parallelism, and then domain specialization
kept the gains coming [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Fifty years of
frontier microprocessor data record that relay directly, with clock frequency
and typical power flattening in the mid-2000s while transistor budgets kept
climbing and core counts turned upward (@fig-microprocessor-trends).

```{python}
#| label: fig-microprocessor-trends
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021.** The data are transcribed from the public dataset maintained by Rupp, which compiles the collection of Horowitz, Labonte, Shacham, Olukotun, Hammond, and Batten. The mid-2000s inflection marks the end of Dennard scaling [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon], followed by greater reliance on parallelism and specialization [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Points are per-4-year frontier maxima from the dataset, not every processor, and era boundaries are approximate.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale line chart of frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021 with five series. Transistors per chip rise from a few thousand to tens of billions by 2021. Single-thread performance rises steeply until the mid-2000s and then slows. Clock frequency plateaus near 3 to 5 GHz and typical power near 100 to 500 W after about 2005, while logical core counts climb from one to the hundreds. Dashed vertical lines mark the approximate end of Dennard scaling around 2005 and the specialization turn around 2016, and a dotted boundary past 2025 marks a prospective fourth era of AI-assisted compositional design, an annotation of this book's argument rather than measured data."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Fifty-year microprocessor trend data; per-series frontier points
# (per-4-year-bucket maxima) transcribed from the public dataset
# github.com/karlrupp/microprocessor-trend-data (50yrs/*.dat, CC-BY 4.0),
# which compiles the Horowitz/Labonte/Shacham/Olukotun/Hammond/Batten
# collection through 2010 plus Rupp's additions through 2021. Values are
# dataset-transcribed, not recomputed; fractional years as in the dataset;
# transistors in thousands, single-thread performance in SpecINT x 1000.
# Era-boundary annotations (~2005 Dennard end, ~2016 specialization turn)
# are approximate: [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon;
# @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Accessed 2026-07-14.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter1-micro-trend.csv
series = [
    {
        "label": "Transistors\n(thousands)",
        "color": COLORS["purple"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 6.1), (1982.3, 136), (1985.9, 274), (1989.5, 1210),
                   (1992.7, 3110), (1998.9, 15300), (2002.9, 221000),
                   (2007.0, 583000), (2009.7, 2.31e6), (2014.8, 5.7e6),
                   (2018.5, 2.11e7), (2021.9, 5.82e7)],
        "label_y": 5.82e7,
    },
    {
        "label": "Single-thread perf\n(SpecINT x 1000)",
        "color": COLORS["blue"],
        "points": [(1988.7, 83.5), (1994.8, 599), (1998.9, 3550),
                   (2002.4, 9390), (2007.0, 35200), (2010.3, 42000),
                   (2014.8, 66000), (2017.6, 83600), (2021.3, 105000)],
        "label_y": 1.05e5,
    },
    {
        "label": "Frequency (MHz)",
        "color": COLORS["orange"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 2.02), (1982.3, 6.1), (1985.9, 16.1),
                   (1988.7, 40.3), (1994.7, 297), (1997.5, 538),
                   (2002.4, 2250), (2004.9, 3650), (2007.7, 4660),
                   (2012.7, 3700), (2016.7, 4000), (2020.9, 3200)],
        "label_y": 3.2e3,
    },
    {
        "label": "Typical power (W)",
        "color": COLORS["red"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 0.922), (1982.3, 3.02), (1986.2, 3.02),
                   (1988.7, 3.96), (1993.0, 35.2), (1998.9, 90.6),
                   (2001.7, 98.2), (2003.4, 155), (2010.4, 168),
                   (2013.4, 300), (2018.5, 250), (2021.9, 500)],
        "label_y": 7.5e2,
    },
    {
        "label": "Logical cores",
        "color": COLORS["green"],
        "points": [(1971.9, 1), (1979.6, 1), (1985.9, 1), (1988.7, 1),
                   (1992.4, 1), (1995.4, 1), (1999.2, 1), (2006.0, 8),
                   (2010.2, 16), (2013.4, 240), (2016.5, 272), (2021.9, 208)],
        "label_y": 1.1e2,
    },
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.075, right=0.775, top=0.90, bottom=0.225)

ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1970, 2032.5)
ax.set_ylim(0.4, 3e9)

for x in (2005, 2016):
    ax.axvline(x, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
               linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)

era_labels = [
    (1987.5, "Device-scaling era"),
    (2010.5, "Parallelism era"),
    (2019.6, "Specialization\nera"),
]
for x, text in era_labels:
    ax.text(x, 6.5e8, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=6.2,
            fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])

# Prospective era: annotation only, no data points and no band fill, styled
# like the other era boundaries but dotted to mark the book's argument.
ax.axvline(2025, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
           linestyle=(0, (1.5, 2.5)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2028.9, 6.5e8, "AI-assisted\ncompositional\ndesign?", ha="center",
        va="center", fontsize=5.4, fontweight="bold",
        color=COLORS["workload_ink"], linespacing=1.2)
ax.text(2028.9, 2.5e7, "(prospective,\nthis book's\nargument)", ha="center",
        va="top", fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.25)

ax.text(2004.3, 4e7, "Dennard scaling\nends", ha="right", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)
ax.text(2016.4, 1.6e6, "specialization\nturn", ha="left", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)

for s in series:
    xs = [p[0] for p in s["points"]]
    ys = [p[1] for p in s["points"]]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=s["color"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=13, color=s["color"], zorder=3)
    ax.text(1.015, s["label_y"], s["label"], ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=s["color"],
            clip_on=False, linespacing=1.15,
            transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform())

ax.set_xticks([1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020])
ax.set_yticks([1, 1e2, 1e4, 1e6, 1e8])
ax.set_yticklabels([r"$10^0$", r"$10^2$", r"$10^4$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^8$"])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Points are per-4-year frontier maxima transcribed from the public "
    "50-year dataset; era boundaries are approximate.",
    xywh=(0.075, 0.03, 0.70, 0.075),
    fontsize=5.6,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Specialization has brought major advances in computer architecture, but it has
also increased complexity. Designs now span more workloads, software
interfaces, and physical constraints, while architects still coordinate much
of the work manually. AI can now assist more of that work, but the limiting
resource varies by study. It may be the number of useful alternatives,
simulator time, interpretation of the results, or evidence strong enough to
support a decision. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
develops that diagnosis in detail. This chapter asks what the broader practice
must record before AI assistance improves architecture rather than only
its output rate.

The quantitative method gave architecture a shared vocabulary for performance,
cost, and power. Architecture 2.0 keeps that quantitative method. It also requires ways
to evaluate the representations, methods, tool paths, and experiments through
which AI participates, because weaknesses in those choices can change the
architectural conclusion.

Turning architectural intent into a tested hardware or software artifact
requires many intermediate decisions. A study should record the relevant
assumptions, allowed changes, tool results, evidence, and rejected candidates so
that humans and AI systems can contribute without obscuring what the result
means.

The purpose of this chapter is to make that shift concrete. We build the
chapter around a single design request, not because the request is already
solvable, but because it reveals the architecture work that any supported
answer would require.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can expand a compact architecture request into a bounded
AI-assisted study. That means you can:

- **Lineage:** Distinguish established uses of machine learning in architecture from broader AI participation across an architecture study.
- **Formulate:** Bound the lighthouse request in terms of workload, baseline, alternatives, constraints, and non-goals.
- **Explore and implement:** Identify meaningful alternatives and the executable artifacts and tool paths needed to compare them.
- **Evaluate and explain:** Specify experiments, counterevidence, and architectural mechanisms that could explain the results.
- **Defend:** State a bounded recommendation, its evidence limits, and the conditions that would reverse it.
- **Efficiency:** Treat efficiency as a multidimensional architecture claim rather than a single score.
:::

## Ask What AI Can Do for Architecture

The phrase "AI for architecture" can be read in a narrow but useful way. A
model can generate text, write scripts, summarize papers, or propose
configurations. The broader question is how the practice of computer
architecture changes when AI can assist the work that connects a question to a
decision.

This broader framing extends rather than replaces an established line of work.
Machine learning has already entered architecture through learned branch
predictors and prefetchers, surrogate models, reinforcement-learning
controllers, predictive design-space exploration, and autotuners. Chen,
Penney, and Jiménez synthesized that generation, organized component by
component, in *AI for Computer Architecture: Principles, Practice, and
Prospects*, a consolidated map of that territory
[@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture]. Predictive design-space exploration
and tensor-program autotuning illustrate this more specific role
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In much of that work,
learning was part of the artifact or a method inside a human-specified
study. These roles remain useful.
The present shift is wider, and this book starts where that map ends. Its
subject is an engineering practice for AI-assisted architecture studies, and
its unit of analysis is the study, not the component. AI may now help frame a problem, propose or edit
mechanisms, call tools, choose experiments, adapt to simulator feedback,
interpret results, and influence which candidate reaches review. Machine
learning is no longer only inside the design. It can also participate in
producing the claim about the design.

Architecture work has always moved through repeated analysis and revision.
Architects frame a problem, choose abstractions, construct models or
simulators, select workloads, explore alternatives, evaluate results, reject
weak candidates, and revise assumptions. Consider an architect weighing a
larger L2 cache for a stated workload mix. The architect might use gem5, a
cycle-level architecture simulator, or FireSim, an FPGA-accelerated full-system
RTL simulation platform, to sweep cache sizes and policies. They would then
compare miss rates, performance, and energy estimates and explain whether locality, contention, or
latency hiding caused the result and state whether the evidence supports a
cache change. Simulators, compilers, profilers, synthesis tools, spreadsheets,
dashboards, and design reviews all mediate that judgment.

AI assistance is useful when it contributes to a specific part of the study.
It may generate candidates, edit artifacts, call tools, summarize evidence,
predict outcomes, search design spaces, critique assumptions, or coordinate
subtasks. To evaluate that contribution, the study must record what the system
could inspect and change, how it obtained feedback, which observations counted
as evidence, and which checks could reject its result.

AI systems are participants in the study, not the study itself. A method may
propose a candidate, estimate an outcome, search alternatives, critique an
assumption, or check a result. A tool is useful when it reports its actions,
observations, costs, and failure modes. Architects or named
engineering teams still set the intent, choose the abstraction and fidelity,
interpret the architectural mechanisms, decide what evidence is adequate, and
define which decisions may be automated. The framework from
@sec-architecture-20-ontology through @sec-feedback-verification-trust
separates these jobs and specifies the interfaces they require.

::: {.column-margin}
**On the term "participant":** It is often singular in the prose and figures, but names an AI-assisted system that a real implementation may realize as one generative method, a pipeline of methods, or several specialized tools. The Architecture 2.0 question is still what each participant may see, change, test, and return for an architect-owned decision.
:::

That division of labor requires a named decision owner.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Name the Decision Owner"}
AI methods can propose and test candidates, and declared automated checks can
reject routine failures. The architect or responsible engineering team sets
the objective, the evidence standard, and the limits on automated approval.
Responsibility for a consequential architecture claim does not pass to the
method that produced it.
:::

With ownership explicit, the permitted AI contribution, tool path, evidence,
and authority boundary together define the practice used throughout this
material.

> **Architecture 2.0.** Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of
> using AI, grounded in architectural representations, tools, and experiments,
> to formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer
> architecture decisions.

AI participation is the premise, not the achievement. The division of labor
remains unchanged, with the bounded architecture study as the
unit of work and the architectural claim as the unit of review. The
representations, tool interfaces, evidence records, rejection checks, and
decision rights introduced later let another architect trace that work without
substituting for the architectural contribution.

@fig-automation-ladder places this wider form of AI participation on the
familiar automation ladder across five eras. The
dates are approximate; the important change is the widening unit of intent and the widening
artifact produced.

1. **1980s--1990s (Logic Synthesis):** Logic synthesis translated Boolean and RTL descriptions into gate-level structure under library, physical, and timing constraints [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization].
2. **1990s--2000s (High-Level Synthesis):** High-level synthesis generated RTL candidates from algorithmic descriptions and moved part of the tactical microarchitecture search into tools [@CoussyMorawiec2008HighLevelSynthesis].
3. **2000s--2010s (Domain-Specific Synthesis):** Building on deductive program synthesis from the 1970s, domain-specific synthesis generated executable code and specialized hardware/software artifacts from mathematical kernels and DSL descriptions [@GulwaniPolozovSingh2017ProgramSynthesis].
4. **2010s--2020s (Agile Hardware Generation):** Agile hardware generation used parameterized templates, such as Chisel and its FIRRTL intermediate representation, to create specialized SoC instances from reusable IP.
5. **2020s onward (AI-Assisted Architecture):** AI can help connect workload intent, software contracts, architectural alternatives, executable tools, experiments, and physical constraints inside a scoped study. The goal is an evidence-backed finding or decision, not a claim that the entire path is automated.

![**Five stages of design automation.** Each stage accepts a broader statement of intent and produces a larger artifact, from Boolean and RTL descriptions that yield gate-level structure to a bounded architecture study that yields an evidence-backed finding or decision. Era spans are approximate and follow the literature cited above.](images/F2b-automation-ladder-timeline){#fig-automation-ladder width="100%" fig-alt="Staircase diagram of five design-automation stages rising from lower left to upper right, each box wider than the one below. From bottom to top: logic synthesis, high-level synthesis, domain-specific synthesis, agile hardware generation, and AI-assisted architecture. A horizontal arrow marks approximate time order and a vertical arrow marks the widening unit of intent and artifact."}

The earlier rungs have mature forms of automation and supply important tools
and artifacts for the broader practice. Each rung also earned adoption only
when its output became independently checkable; designers trusted synthesized
netlists once static timing analysis and equivalence checking could reject bad
ones. Architecture 2.0 asks how AI can help an architect connect the rungs
while preserving the meaning and limits of the result.

This material uses the architecture study as its unit of work. Its design loop
repeats proposal, evaluation, rejection or revision, and a decision about what
to do next. Recording those steps lets another architect inspect how the study
was carried out.

The framework has three practical uses.

- **Researcher:** Describe an AI-for-architecture paper by naming the
  architecture question, the role AI played, the artifact or finding produced,
  and the evidence and limits behind it.
- **Tool builder:** Ask whether a harness records enough state for another
  method or team to learn from it.
- **Author or reviewer:** Ask what would reject the claim, why the measured
  mechanism supports it, and which decision it can justify.

The framework from @sec-architecture-20-ontology through
@sec-feedback-verification-trust develops reusable reports and review checks
for those needs.

## From Architecture 1.0 to Architecture 2.0 {#sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20}

Architecture 1.0 and Architecture 2.0 distinguish modes of participation, not
an obsolete practice from a superior one. Architecture 1.0 is shorthand for
the familiar practice of human-orchestrated design. The architect defines the
problem, chooses models, uses tools, interprets feedback, and decides what to
build. This practice is the foundation on which the field stands.

Architecture 2.0 allows AI to participate across more of the study. The
architect remains responsible for intent, constraints, abstraction, evidence standards,
mechanism-level interpretation, and the final decision. Broader AI participation
also creates a practical obligation. The study must represent enough state,
permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejection for another
architect to understand what happened and what the result can support.

The difference can be seen in a familiar design-space exploration. In
human-orchestrated practice, an architect might manually script a simulator sweep over
cache sizes, associativities, and replacement policies, then inspect the
results. In an AI-assisted study, a
method proposes candidates, a surrogate[^fn-ml-surrogate-model] estimates outcomes, a simulator
evaluates selected points, a critic flags invalid assumptions, and a human
decides whether the evidence is strong enough. The contribution may be a cache
artifact, an architectural finding, a reusable method or dataset, or a
decision supported by the available evidence. Because the study records the
loop, another architect can trace how that contribution was produced. The loop does
not determine what the contribution must be.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-model]: A surrogate model is a cheap, data-driven stand-in for an expensive simulator. In a study it lets a method screen many candidates quickly, but its score is only a proxy. A promising candidate still has to be escalated to higher-fidelity evidence before commitment.

The practical difference lies in what the study must record. The left loop
shows familiar human-orchestrated practice, where architectural judgment
coordinates intent, models, candidates, tool runs, and expert review
(@fig-design-loop-breaks). The right loop does not remove that judgment. It represents enough state, permitted actions, tool
feedback, evidence, and rejection for AI methods to participate
without becoming an uninspectable prompt-to-chip shortcut.

![**AI participation changes what an architecture study must record.** A represented execution loop records state, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejection that otherwise remain part of informal human practice.](images/F2a-design-loop-breaks){#fig-design-loop-breaks width="100%" fig-alt="Two side-by-side loop diagrams compare a human-orchestrated architecture study with an AI-assisted study whose execution records state, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, and rejection."}

The execution record exists so that others can inspect the work. A participant,
whether one method or a
coordinated set of methods, is not the whole study. Its actions must connect to
versioned representations, tool interfaces, evidence records, and named
decision points. Those connections let another architect inspect how an
artifact or finding was produced and whether it supports the proposed claim.

::: {#pri-design-the-loop .callout-design-principle title="Design the study, not only the generator"}
Judge an AI-assisted result by the study that produced it, not by the generated
artifact alone. Record the question, alternatives, permitted actions, tool
results, rejected candidates, evidence, and decision so another architect can
see why the result supports the claim.
:::

A useful AI-assisted system needs more than simulator calls, RTL proposals, or
paper summaries. The surrounding study should follow the habits of sound
systems engineering. It
should suspect the bottleneck, account for memory
movement and physical limits, compare tradeoffs numerically, preserve
provenance, record failure modes, and ask what evidence is strong enough for
the next commitment. Those habits do not appear automatically because a model
is capable. They have to be designed into the study and its tool path.

## The Architecture Moonshot

Before returning to the concrete prompt as an architecture object, we need
clear definitions of two terms, *architecture* and *architect*. In
this book, architecture does not mean only microarchitecture, a block diagram,
or a chip artifact.

> **Architecture.** Architecture is the hardware-software contract and system
> organization that turn workload intent and technology constraints into a
> defensible system design. It includes instruction set architecture (ISA) and microarchitecture, memory and
> interconnect, accelerators and chiplets, compiler/runtime interfaces,
> physical-design constraints, verification, deployment, and the evidence used
> to justify choices.

> **Architect.** The architect is responsible for the decision. The role frames the problem,
> chooses the abstractions, sets the evidence
> standard, defines what can reject a candidate, and remains answerable for the
> committed design. One person, an engineering team, or several authorized
> review roles may perform that work, but the study must name their decision
> rights and who gives final approval. AI methods contribute inside the
> study. The architect remains responsible for the synthesis and the architecture claim.

With those terms fixed, we can read the lighthouse prompt as more than a
compact design request. It asks for a compute subsystem serving real-time
mobile extended reality (XR), under a thermal-design-power (TDP) budget in a
low-power (LP) mobile process class, and it asks for a report rather than a
chip. Its purpose here is diagnostic. The request is deliberately beyond
present end-to-end capability because answering it would require a complete
study.

The canonical prompt leaves more specific accelerator, memory, and integration
choices open so that one sentence can travel through the book unchanged.

> **Lighthouse prompt.** The lighthouse prompt is the compact design request
> above. It is quoted unchanged throughout the book and requires a design-space
> report with evidence and rejected alternatives.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="Read the Prompt as an AI-Assisted Architecture Study"}
**Context.** The lighthouse prompt is not a miniature product specification. We
use it to identify the work that a complete AI-assisted architecture study
would require.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem" sets the
ISA/ABI and software contract the method must respect. "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload" sets
the workload scope the AI method must optimize. "vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block" sets the
compute organization bounds for the method's action space. "3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile
process" sets the physical-design envelope that can reject a generated
proposal, and "design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives"
sets the evidence standard the study owes the architect. Together, those
fragments require the study to account for memory, data movement, the compiler,
the runtime, reliability, and verification rather than treating the prompt as
a simple hardware-generation task.

Treat the prompt as a recurring stress test. A proposed study has not yet
answered it unless it states the question, alternatives, artifacts,
experiments, explanation, recommendation, evidence limits, and decision owner.
:::

The sentence introduced at the start of the chapter is quoted fragment by
fragment throughout the book, and @fig-moonshot-prompt keeps it available as an
object of analysis. The top panel restates the prompt; the middle panel names the
architectural obligations embedded in that request; and the bottom panel shows
the execution and analysis turn needed before an AI system's answer deserves
architectural trust. The study must represent the task, restrict the tool calls
and method actions, gather evidence, reject weak outputs, explain the result,
decide what to commit, and revise the next turn. The figure shows the work a
supported answer requires.

![**The lighthouse prompt expands into an architecture study:** It surfaces the architecture obligations and the execution machinery needed to formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a supported answer.](images/F1-moonshot-stack){#fig-moonshot-prompt width="100%" fig-alt="Stacked diagram with the full lighthouse prompt at the top, architecture obligations in the middle, and the represented execution and analysis requirements needed before a result can be trusted at the bottom."}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

The book keeps the mobile XR prompt as its single lighthouse so one request can
accumulate detail. It intentionally exceeds present end-to-end capability. Its
purpose is to identify the hidden state, evidence, and decisions that a
plausible answer could otherwise conceal. This book argues for building the surrounding
engineering now through representations, tools, experiments, and rejection
paths instead of waiting for a larger model.

With that concrete prompt in view, the word *moonshot* can be used precisely.
The term should not mean a prediction that the field can already automate
architecture end to end. X, the Moonshot Factory, frames a moonshot as the
intersection of a huge problem, a radical solution, and a breakthrough
technology that makes the solution plausible enough to pursue
[@XCompanyMoonshotBlueprint]. This book adapts that structure to computer
architecture.

Use the moonshot analogy only to name a field-building requirement. A hard
target is useful only if work toward it produces shared tasks, instruments,
methods, and evidence standards that outlive one flagship result. Computer architecture has
done this before. The Mead and Conway VLSI methodology joined abstractions and
design rules to tools, fabrication access, and pedagogy
[@MeadConway1980VLSI], while the reduced-instruction-set program reshaped the
hardware/software contract around quantitative evidence
[@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. Both changed how a community worked, not only the
artifact it produced.

Architecture 2.0 earns a place in that lineage only if its representations,
tools, experiments, and review practices become useful beyond the projects that
introduced them. The aim is a process that others can run, challenge, and
improve, not a gallery of exceptional demonstrations.

> **Architecture moonshot.** An architecture moonshot is a difficult, long-term
> target that ordinary ad hoc coordination cannot evaluate credibly. It aims to
> improve substantially how AI-assisted architecture work is formulated,
> explored, implemented, evaluated, explained, and defended. Advances in AI,
> data, or tools must also make at least part of the target practical to study
> without suggesting that the whole problem is solved.

The same pattern appears in the architecture vocabulary used here, as @fig-moonshot-venn shows.
The phrase is deliberately about the field's capacity, not about dismissing classical
architecture practice. Classical architecture already has models, measurements,
review, and signoff procedures. The moonshot is to document and connect enough of
that practice to tools and evidence that AI methods can participate
without hiding what their output means or what would reject it.

![**A moonshot needs three conditions at once:** An architecture moonshot sits at the intersection of a grand architecture challenge, a radical change in AI-assisted architecture practice, and an enabling AI/data/tool breakthrough that makes the target plausible enough to study.](images/F1c-moonshot-venn){#fig-moonshot-venn width="100%" fig-alt="Three-circle Venn diagram showing an architecture moonshot at the intersection of a grand challenge, a radical practice target, and an enabling breakthrough."}

This architecture-specific moonshot is worth naming because the pressure is
real but the solution is not yet settled. Hardware/software systems now span
workloads, software stacks, ISAs, microarchitecture, accelerators, memory
systems, Electronic Design Automation (EDA), physical constraints,
verification, and deployment. A credible AI-assisted process must connect
these parts while recording evidence, failures, and the conditions that reject
a candidate. Recent AI methods, architecture datasets, and tool interfaces make
portions of such studies possible. Publicly reported examples already include reinforcement
learning[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning] systems for physical-design exploration and learned circuit-generation
loops with silicon evidence
[@Synopsys2023DSOai; @RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL]. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack returns to those examples
as cases in different operating regimes. These examples show methods and tool paths with evidence,
not end-to-end architecture autonomy. AgentDSE, a recent LLM-agent system for
simulator-in-the-loop design-space exploration, adds the same lesson in a DSE
setting. It delegates pieces of architectural reasoning while simulator feedback
keeps the search grounded [@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE]. The remaining research
problem is how to combine these capabilities in studies that people can review
and remain responsible for.

[^fn-rl-reinforcement-learning]: Reinforcement learning is a machine-learning method in which an agent learns to choose actions that maximize a cumulative reward signal from its environment, often used to navigate complex design spaces [@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. In an AI-assisted architecture study, it is one search method, powerful for exploration but still owing the same evidence and checks as any other proposal source.

Generating more candidates does not by itself advance computer architecture.
SPEC-style benchmarks
made performance claims more comparable by specifying workloads, run rules,
and reporting standards. Shared simulators such as gem5 made
architecture experiments easier to inspect across labs. FPGA-accelerated
simulation platforms such as FireSim, community benchmark suites such as MLPerf,
artifact-evaluation practices, and open tool flows have mattered because they
let other researchers understand, rerun, challenge, and safely build on
reported results [@SPEC2017CPU; @BinkertEtAl2011Gem5; @KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim;
@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf].

AlphaChip provides a real case against which to test these requirements. It is a
learning method for macro placement on production netlists, a narrowly defined
physical-design task [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. Google reports using
it in the design of tensor processing units
[@GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum; @GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip]. The result also
prompted a public disagreement about baselines, proxy fidelity, compute, and
reproducibility [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @Markov2023FalseDawn]. A later
assessment evaluates the released code and pretrained checkpoint with stronger
simulated annealing, public sub-10-nm cases, and commercial post-route metrics
[@ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment]. The proprietary production blocks and
original pretraining corpus remain unavailable for independent reproduction
from the public record.

@sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration examines the proxy question,
and @sec-alphachip-public-record-audit reviews the public record in more detail.
The purpose is not to dismiss the work. AlphaChip is influential, reported as
deployed, and genuinely contested, which makes it useful for examining the
evidence requirements that any AI-assisted architecture claim must meet.

::: {.callout-war-story title="What the AlphaChip public record establishes"}
**The claim.** In 2021, a Google team reported a reinforcement-learning method that generates
a chip's macro placement in under six hours, at quality
comparable to or better than human experts on key power, performance, and area
metrics [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. It was named AlphaChip in a 2024
*Nature* addendum [@GoldieEtAl2024AlphaChipAddendum] and, Google later said,
used across several TPU generations [@GoogleDeepMind2024AlphaChip].

**The gap.** Independent groups challenged the reported advantage. A 2023 reassessment with
open code and shared test cases found that a strong
simulated-annealing baseline matched or beat the method's proxy cost on most
modern test cases, that commercial and analytical placers often produced lower
routed wirelength, and that the proxy correlated weakly with final chip metrics
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. The original authors replied that the assessment
omitted central pretraining, stopped before convergence, used much less compute,
and tested designs they considered unrepresentative
[@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. Google later released code and a pretrained
checkpoint, but not the proprietary inputs used for pretraining. In September
2024, *Nature* removed an earlier editor's note, stated that its concerns had
been resolved, and published a clarifying addendum. That resolved the journal's
process but did not produce an independent public reproduction of the strongest
pretraining and deployment claims. The reassessment's authors reaffirmed their
conclusions in a 2026 journal update [@ChengEtAl2026UpdatedAssessment]. As of
July 2026, the CACM version of a separate critique
[@Markov2023FalseDawn] has an ACM expression of concern concerning its content
and disclosure. An expression of concern records an open inquiry, not a finding.

**The lesson.** Google reports deployment across TPU generations, but no independent
comparison has tested the complete method and strong classical
baselines under the same conditions. This material does not attempt to resolve
the dispute. The case shows why consequential AI-assisted architecture claims
need shared benchmarks, reproducible artifacts where possible, and an accepted
review process.
:::

Returning to the lighthouse prompt, each fragment sets a specific constraint. XRBench, a benchmark suite for mobile XR workloads, gives the prompt a workload anchor rather than a vague application
label [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench]. End-to-end XR systems research has shown how
tightly the stages of the full perception-to-display pipeline couple, which is
what makes mobile XR a demanding architecture target rather than a single
kernel to optimize [@HuzaifaEtAl2021ILLIXR]. The node is
intentionally stated as a class rather than as a named foundry PDK (process design kit); current
mobile SoCs are publicly
described in 3 nm-class technology, but an architecture study
must still state which process, libraries, voltage assumptions, and signoff
path it actually uses [@Apple2024A18Pro; @Apple2024M4].

One iteration might ingest an XRBench trace slice and software pipeline,
propose vector-width, local-memory, or CPU/accelerator alternatives, screen them
with a cheap proxy, and preserve each rejection. A later turn can spend scarce
compiler, simulator, RTL, or physical-design feedback only where it resolves
the architectural decision.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Allocating Expensive Evaluation"}
Before a study transitions from proxy evidence to an expensive simulator
or physical-design evaluation, a declared allocation rule should determine
which candidates advance. Routine checks can be automated; the responsible
team defines the rule, reviews exceptions, and decides what claims the resulting
evidence may support. Costlier feedback does not authorize a stronger claim by
itself.
:::

The book returns to this prompt without making it the only example. Later
chapters examine facets of the same request: memory and data movement,
software drift, chiplet partitioning, verification, physical design, and
deployment. Additional examples appear only when a facet needs a more specific
study. Each return restates the workload, comparison, physical envelope, and
evidence obligations so that a changing method is not mistaken for a
changing architectural claim.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Why the Prompt Spans the Stack {#sec-why-the-prompt-spans-the-stack}

The prompt is architectural because each phrase creates obligations beyond the
surface words. A study must track workload behavior, software
contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, evidence, and rejection
paths together. That obligation stays compact in @tbl-moonshot-decomposition, a
reader's checklist for why the prompt crosses boundaries that architecture
cannot ignore rather than a shopping list of subtasks. The
workload phrase says what behavior the design must serve. The ISA and compute
phrases say what boundary the hardware/software interface must specify. The
power and process phrase says what physical world can reject the idea. The
report phrase says what kind of evidence the study owes the architect before
the answer deserves trust. Each row therefore names both a design decision and
a way for the study to be wrong.

| **Prompt fragment** | **Architectural decisions** | **Evidence or rejection need** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **XRBench mobile XR** | Workload slice, input distribution, quality-of-service target, latency deadline, memory traffic, software pipeline, and drift assumptions. | Trace provenance, benchmark version, documented workload-phase selection, and rejection when results over-optimize a single hot loop or miss real-time behavior. |
| **64-bit RISC-V with vector or accelerator option** | ISA boundary, custom extension policy, programming model, compiler/runtime path, library support, and software compatibility. | Correctness, toolchain support, generated-code evidence, portability checks, and rejection of unsupported software semantics. |
| **Compute subsystem** | CPU, fixed-function accelerator, reconfigurable spatial array (e.g., CGRA or Reconfigurable Dataflow Architecture/RDA), or Processing-in-Memory (PIM) data-centric block; memory hierarchy, SoC integration, interface compatibility, and relevant reliability or isolation assumptions. | Design-space comparison and rejection of candidates that only win by moving cost, bandwidth, energy, complexity, or risk elsewhere. |
| **3 W, 3 nm-class low-power envelope** | Power, voltage/frequency, thermal, area, EDA constraints, and dynamic power management interfaces (e.g., Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling or DVFS, power domains, thermal sensors) for the OS/firmware. | Power-model provenance, physical-design limits (IR drop, electromigration, timing closure), sensitivity analysis, and predeclared rejection criteria for runtime throttling or reliability failures within the scoped workload. |
| **Design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives** | Alternatives, the Pareto frontier (the objective outcomes of feasible designs for which no alternative is at least as good on every objective and strictly better on at least one) [@Deb2001MultiObjective], assumptions, uncertainty, verification plan, rejected or failed candidates, and decision points with named owners. | Connected measurements, assumptions, checks, rejections, coverage, reproducible artifacts, and a named owner for advancing the claim. |

: **Prompt fragments create architecture obligations:** The lighthouse prompt maps to decisions about workload definition, software and ISA contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, and evidence. {#tbl-moonshot-decomposition tbl-colwidths="[18,34,35]"}

The table is a diagnostic, not the book's final schema. It lets a reader reject
a weak lighthouse answer immediately when the answer cannot identify its
workload slice, software contract, physical assumptions, supporting
observations, or named decision owner.
The discussions from @sec-data-representations-world-models through
@sec-feedback-verification-trust give these obligations precise
representations, tool interfaces, evidence practices, and governance rules.
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card eventually consolidates them
in a compact review form, after the reader has seen why each field is needed.

::: {.callout-engineer-move title="Identify the Hidden Obligations"}
Expand any compact architecture request by answering six questions. For the
lighthouse prompt, @tbl-moonshot-decomposition supplies the first answers.

1. **What architecture question is being formulated?** Name the workload,
   baseline, constraints, scope, and non-goals.
2. **Which meaningful alternatives should be explored?** Separate choices that
   could change the conclusion from trivial parameter variations.
3. **What artifacts and tool path will implement them?** Name the models,
   software, simulators, RTL, or physical-design steps needed for comparison.
4. **Which experiments and counterevidence will evaluate them?** State the
   measurements, fidelity, cost, and failure conditions.
5. **Which architectural mechanisms could explain the result?** Connect the
   measurements to workload behavior, hardware organization, and software.
6. **What recommendation can be defended?** State who is responsible for it, what its limits
   are, and what evidence would reverse it.

A request that cannot answer these questions is not yet ready to serve as an
AI-assisted architecture study.
:::

No single model can make these obligations disappear. The table is deliberately
compressed; each row expands into many implementation and evidence questions.
Meeting the "3 W, 3 nm-class" constraint requires work in RTL, synthesis,
floorplanning, timing, IR drop, leakage, thermal analysis, and signoff.
Evaluating the "RISC-V with vector or accelerator option" alternatives also
requires compiler, runtime, library, generated-code, and portability evidence. Architecture development
therefore means proposing artifacts, predicting consequences, optimizing under
constraints, and rejecting weak evidence across changing fidelity levels. This
is why the moonshot is a computer architecture problem rather than prompt
engineering. The study has to connect architectural state across the stack.

## More Than Candidate Generation {#sec-architecture-development-spans-three-roles}

The lighthouse prompt also shows why candidate generation cannot stand for the
whole design process. Different methods may propose an ISA extension,
estimate its latency and energy, search nearby alternatives, critique its
software assumptions, repair an invalid artifact, or verify a result.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization later defines the full set of
method roles and their interactions; these six jobs are the subset this
chapter needs. Here, the narrower point is
that no one kind of automated work can discharge all the obligations in
@tbl-moonshot-decomposition.

The six capabilities describe architecture work. Generation, prediction,
optimization, critique, repair, and verification are specific jobs that methods
may perform in support of that work. A method job is not a learner outcome, and
no combination of such jobs automatically produces an architecture explanation
or decision.

Producing a candidate is only one part of an architecture study.
Three method jobs, generation, prediction, and optimization, are previewed in
@fig-architecture-development-triad, and critique, repair, and verification are
left to the prose that follows. This is a preview, not a taxonomy the reader
must master in this chapter.

![**Method roles serve a broader architecture study:** Generation proposes artifacts and candidates, prediction estimates behavior and risk, and optimization searches tradeoffs under constraints. These jobs support the six architecture capabilities but do not replace evidence, mechanism-level explanation, or human architectural judgment.](images/F1b-architecture-development-triad){#fig-architecture-development-triad width="100%" fig-alt="Three-circle diagram showing generation, prediction, and optimization as interacting method jobs within a broader AI-assisted architecture study."}

For the mobile XR example, a proposed vector extension remains unsupported
until evaluation estimates its behavior under the power envelope and compares
it with an accelerator, a memory-system change, or a software change. A proxy
may rank candidates one way, a simulator may overturn that ranking, and an EDA
run may return timing paths, congestion, or rule violations rather than a
single score. Search can also overfit[^fn-ml-overfitting] a convenient proxy.
The study must connect these different kinds of work and preserve the reasons a
candidate advanced or failed.

[^fn-ml-overfitting]: Overfitting, in machine learning, is fitting the noise and idiosyncrasies of a training sample so that performance fails to generalize to new data. An optimizer can overfit a proxy the same way, raising the proxy's measured score by exploiting its quirks rather than improving the true objective. The study therefore escalates a promising candidate to stronger evidence before commitment.

The jobs interact, but they do not form a fixed pipeline. Generation can reveal
a missing constraint. Prediction can show that the current evidence is too weak
to separate two candidates. Optimization can allocate scarce simulator or EDA
runs, while critique and verification can disqualify the apparent winner.
Repair changes the artifact and may invalidate measurements collected before
the change. The study therefore preserves the consequential state of the work,
including candidates, assumptions, measurements, rejected alternatives, and
the claims those records can support. It does not need every internal model
trace.

In the mobile XR study, generation might produce legal vector configurations,
prediction might estimate their data-movement and power risks, and optimization
might decide which configurations merit expensive evaluation. Those methods
can narrow the work, but they cannot determine whether a compiler assumption
holds, whether the measured behavior follows from the proposed mechanism,
or whether the remaining tradeoff justifies a commitment. Those are properties
of the broader architecture study.



## Efficiency Claims Need Valid Comparisons {#sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops}

The prompt defines several obligations, but the study still needs a specific
claim. An efficiency study asks how well a particular architecture performs
useful work with limited resources. Its result should be a testable claim about
that design, workload, and resource budget, not merely an artifact. A design
that is faster but consumes too much power, is impossible to verify, or depends
on fragile software assumptions has not really solved the architectural problem.
If Architecture 2.0 only made architects produce more artifacts, it would not be
enough. The goal is to produce better, more reliable, and more efficient systems
under rising complexity.

When candidate generation becomes cheap, evaluation and review may become the
bottleneck. That diagnosis must be measured rather than inferred from one
failure. The team should compare how quickly valid proposals arrive with how
quickly it can test them, reject weak ones, explain the results, and make a
decision.

The hard shift is not from performance to a single new metric called power. It
is that efficiency itself is becoming more multidimensional. Classical computer
architecture made performance quantitative, but it also treated cost and power
as first-class constraints
[@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Dennard-style scaling once
made it easier to improve performance while keeping power density manageable
[@DennardEtAl1974Scaling]. As that story weakened, dark silicon (the share of a chip that must stay powered off to fit its thermal budget) and the limits
of multicore scaling pushed the field toward specialization
[@BorkarChien2011FutureMicroprocessors; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
Data-movement energy made arithmetic alone an insufficient efficiency story
[@Horowitz2014Energy]. Warehouse-scale operation expanded the boundary to
power delivery, utilization, networking, operations, and total cost of
ownership [@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer].
Sustainability adds another layer because carbon depends on operational
energy, hardware manufacturing and infrastructure, utilization, geography, and
lifetime
[@GuptaEtAl2021ChasingCarbon].
An AI-assisted study must record the assumptions and evidence relevant to each
efficiency dimension and state when a violation should reject a candidate.

Given this multidimensional efficiency, the question is not whether traditional architecture methods suddenly stop
working. Many still work extremely well when the workload, abstraction,
feedback path, and proposed transition are specified. The harder question is where
human-orchestrated work becomes too slow, too implicit, or too expensive to
manage the coupled objectives. Architecture 2.0 should record that boundary,
separating the parts that familiar models, scripts, simulation, and
expert review can still handle from the parts that benefit from AI-assisted
formulation, search, implementation, evaluation, explanation, or review.

A valid comparison must also record what failed, not only the winners.
Recording failed and rejected alternatives prevents later teams from repeating
work the study already ruled out and shows which parts of the design space the claim
actually covers. @sec-data-representations-world-models develops the
representation, and @sec-feedback-verification-trust develops the evidence
practice.

Modern benchmark suites show this same pressure toward multidimensional efficiency. MLPerf, the community benchmark effort for
making machine-learning performance claims reproducible across systems, treats
deployment scenario, latency, throughput, accuracy, and power as part of the
comparison rather than as one scalar claim [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf].
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales develops that case with the numbers; the
architectural lesson here is that efficiency is a structured claim about useful
work under constraints, not one number. Useful work must be
evaluated against the relevant scarce resource, such as latency, throughput, energy,
power, area, dollar cost, carbon, reliability, verification effort, engineering
time, or risk.

Every efficiency claim must identify the design, workload, scenario, and scarce
resource used to define useful work. Changing any one changes the claim and the
evidence needed to support it. @tbl-efficiency-dimensions summarizes the coupled
obligations a valid comparison must represent and test.

| **Dimension** | **Efficiency question** | **Why it complicates the comparison** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Performance** | How much useful work is delivered per unit time, latency budget, or service-level target? | The answer depends on workload selection, scenario, software stack, and whether the measured behavior matches the deployment claim. |
| **Power and energy** | How much useful work is delivered per watt, joule, thermal budget, or battery envelope? | The study must model activity, data movement, voltage/frequency choices, thermal constraints, and fidelity gaps between estimates and signoff. |
| **Reliability and correctness** | How much useful work survives faults, corner cases, nondeterminism, and validation? | A faster candidate is not efficient if it spends its savings on fragility, debug burden, or invalid software and hardware assumptions. |
| **Scalability and cost** | How much useful work is delivered per dollar, rack, network hop, operator action, or unit of capacity? | Local wins can shift cost to memory, network, power delivery, utilization, operations, or total cost of ownership. |
| **Sustainability** | How much useful work is delivered per unit of operational and embodied environmental footprint? | Carbon depends on hardware lifetime, manufacturing, energy mix, utilization, and where and when computation runs. |
| **Evidence and engineering effort** | How much decision-relevant evidence is obtained per simulation, experiment, verification run, or engineer-hour? | A study that generates more candidates can still be inefficient if it consumes scarce feedback, hides failures, or produces evidence that cannot reject outputs. |

: **Efficiency is becoming multidimensional:** Performance, power, reliability, scalability, sustainability, and evidence cost are increasingly coupled. The architecture question is how a study compares them without hiding shifted cost or changed scope. {#tbl-efficiency-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[22,32,34]"}

The word efficient should therefore be read broadly. A candidate that improves
simulated performance while increasing verification burden may not be
efficient. A candidate that reduces energy but requires fragile software
assumptions may not be efficient. A candidate that looks good under a proxy
metric but fails under a more faithful workload may not be efficient.
Architecture 2.0 should treat efficiency as a scoped architecture claim, not a
property inferred from one attractive artifact or score.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. The requested subsystem must
support a workload class, meet a power envelope, fit a technology assumption,
interact with software, and produce evidence. The study must compare
tradeoffs among energy, latency, memory traffic, programmability,
verification, and deployment risk. A single scalar objective may be useful
inside the study, but it cannot be the whole architectural judgment.

## Boundaries of the Argument

With supported efficiency claims set as the target, the boundaries deserve
equal clarity. This book is not a paper catalog. The field is moving too quickly
for a catalog to remain useful for long. The goal is to give readers a
framework that can organize current work and still be useful as models, tools,
and benchmarks change.

Nor is the goal to make a product forecast. The book does not claim that a
particular model, tool harness, simulator, EDA flow, or benchmark will define
the field. Those will evolve. The lasting questions are which architecture
decision the work advances, how AI contributes, and what evidence and limits
make the claim defensible.

Nor is this a tool manual. Tools matter deeply, but the focus is how they
support architecture questions, artifacts, experiments, explanations, and
decisions rather than installation instructions or process recipes.
@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping gives a compact bootstrap path.
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives a compact review form and rubric.

It is also not a claim that AI systems replace architects. As AI participation
widens, architectural responsibility changes rather than disappears. An
architect or responsible engineering team must frame the task, choose
abstractions, representations, tools, and fidelity, interpret mechanisms, set
evidence standards, define what may be automated, and remain answerable for
consequential claims. @sec-data-representations-world-models develops the
records that document such work.

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="What Not to Claim"}
Architecture 2.0 should not be read as push-button chip design, replacement of
architects, or a claim that today's generative methods define the field. The
defensible claim is broader than one method and narrower than autonomous chip design.
Appropriate AI assistance, grounded in architecture representations, tools,
and experiments, can improve how architects formulate problems, explore and
implement alternatives, evaluate and explain results, and defend decisions.
:::

The rest of the book develops that approach. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
diagnoses where rising specialization and system coupling create useful roles
for AI and where they merely move the bottleneck. @sec-architecture-20-ontology
defines the study, claim, loop, and supporting instruments.
@sec-data-representations-world-models and
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces develop the representations,
data, environments, and tool interfaces needed for implementation.
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization separates method roles,
and @sec-feedback-verification-trust develops evaluation and evidence.
@sec-running-the-loop executes and analyzes the book's model-plus-SCALE-Sim
study. @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack tests transfer
across architecture layers, and @sec-what-architect-owns integrates the six
capabilities into a defensible recommendation. The appendices supply practical
review artifacts and a resource catalog.

## Open Research Questions

These open research questions set the field-level agenda for the rest of the
material. They are intentionally broad because this chapter is the entry point.
The discussion from @sec-architecture-20-ontology through
@sec-what-architect-owns makes state, environments, methods, evidence,
execution, and responsibility more concrete.

1. **When is AI-assisted design work an architecture contribution?**
AI-assisted work becomes architectural when it changes what can be credibly
understood, built, compared, or decided under architectural constraints. A
research rubric here would distinguish architectural contributions in
artifacts, methods, datasets, environments, findings, and decisions from
novelty in the AI method alone. A strong evaluation would test reviewer agreement on borderline papers
and show where the rubric changes review outcomes. This question uses the
different modes of participation in @sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20.

2. **How should venues review AI-assisted architecture studies?**
Architecture venues already know how to inspect artifacts, baselines, and
quantitative tradeoffs. AI-assisted studies add represented state, permitted
actions, method roles, rejected alternatives, and commitment boundaries to the
evidence behind an architectural claim.
A paper or community study could propose review criteria, artifact-review
norms, and call-for-papers language for this new class of work, then test
whether reviewers identify unsupported commitments earlier. The moonshot
framing makes this a community problem, not a matter of
individual paper style.

3. **What shared infrastructure makes progress comparable?**
If AI-assisted architecture remains a collection of isolated demonstrations,
the field will struggle to compare results. Shared problem and case packets,
datasets, workload suites, environments, baselines, experiment protocols,
explanation criteria, rejected-alternative records, and instructor-ready
studies would let groups produce contrastable findings without pretending every
architecture problem has one fixed benchmark. A field-shaping moonshot should
be judged by whether these artifacts survive the flagship result and enable
follow-on work, negative results, and fair rejection.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked what it would take for AI to help an architect formulate,
explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a system design rather than
settle for a plausible candidate. The chapter develops the compact lighthouse
prompt into an architecture study with a question, meaningful alternatives,
checkable artifacts, experiments, a mechanism-level explanation, and a
recommendation. The study is the unit of work, and its architectural claim is
the unit another architect reviews.

That shift is forced by efficiency, now read as a coupled budget across
performance, energy, power delivery, reliability, sustainability, cost,
verification burden, and time to decision-relevant evidence, rather than a single number.
When plausible artifacts are cheap, the scarce work is the burden of proof, and
the limiting factor may shift among search, tool feedback, interpretation,
evidence, and decision. A represented design loop helps execute and inspect
that work. It is one process within the practice described here.

None of this removes the architect, nor does it require a person to approve
every routine check. The architect or responsible engineering team frames the
task, chooses abstractions, representations, tools, and experiments, explains
the architectural mechanisms, sets evidence standards, declares what may be
automated, and remains responsible for the resulting claim. Architecture 2.0
uses AI to improve architecture work while recording the path from question to
decision well enough for others to challenge and improve.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Read any AI architecture result as a study with declared limits. Identify its question, alternatives, artifacts, experiments, mechanism-level explanation, recommendation, and evidence limits.
- **Reader test:** Can you reconstruct how AI contributed across those obligations well enough to challenge the architecture claim and its reversal conditions?
- **Up next:** @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales asks where AI genuinely improves that work and where it merely moves the bottleneck.
:::
